Field Guide · Systems
Vol. 1  —  Peer-to-Peer Networks

TETRON

Anatomy of a mesh VPN daemon: how encrypted tunnels, virtual interfaces, and lock-free routing tables move a packet from one machine to another with no server in between.

A structural survey of the tetron daemon · Rust / QUIC / iroh

Tetron is a peer-to-peer mesh VPN daemon. It creates a virtual network adapter on the host, assigns it an IP inside a private subnet, and routes packets to other members of that subnet over direct, encrypted tunnels — no central server sits in the data path. Each node authenticates with an Ed25519 identity key; each tunnel is a QUIC connection encrypted with ChaCha20-Poly1305.

A single relay node, run by the project rather than by any member, exists only to help nodes find each other and punch through NAT. Once that handshake completes, packets travel directly between the two endpoints. If a direct path is impossible, the relay forwards encrypted traffic without being able to read it.

The daemon manages this per network, per peer, concurrently, without a single global lock. What follows is a structural survey: the shape of the code, the path a packet takes, and the state it touches along the way.

01

The Daemon's Skeleton

One process, many networks. A single MeshManager owns every network the host has joined, each isolated in its own NetworkHandle with its own routing table, membership roster, and TUN interface.

Fig. 2 — Structural Blocks
Every joined network gets its own NetworkHandle: its own subnet, its own TUN device, its own peer table. A misbehaving or compromised network cannot see traffic on another. The PeerTable is the one component on the hot path — it never blocks.

MeshManager is the top-level daemon object. It holds the QUIC Endpoint, the node's Ed25519 identity, and a DashMap of every active NetworkHandle, keyed by network name. It serves the local IPC socket that the CLI and GUI clients speak to.

NetworkHandle is one joined network. It carries the node's assigned IP inside that network's subnet, a handle to the TUN device, the peer routing table, and a reference to the network's shared, lock-protected state.

NetworkState holds what changes slowly: the member roster, the approved-member list, the subnet definition, and outstanding invites. It is wrapped in a read-write lock, since reads (status queries) vastly outnumber writes (roster updates).

PeerTable holds what changes fast: which virtual IP maps to which live QUIC connection. It is backed by a lock-free concurrent map, because every forwarded packet touches it.

02

The Path of a Packet

The data plane is three concurrent tasks per network, connected by channels rather than shared locks. None of them block on the others.

01

run_mesh — outbound

Reads a raw IP packet off the TUN device, parses the destination address, looks it up in the PeerTable, and sends it as a QUIC datagram over the matching connection.

02

spawn_peer_reader — inbound

One per connected peer. Receives QUIC datagrams, checks that the packet's source IP actually matches that peer's cryptographic identity, then hands valid packets to the TUN write channel.

03

spawn_tun_writer — ingestion

Drains the TUN write channel and writes packets to the host's virtual interface, where the local application receives them.

Fig. 3 — Forwarding Pipeline, One Network
Outbound and inbound run as separate task chains linked only by channels. A slow peer connection cannot stall the TUN writer; a slow TUN write cannot stall the network reader.
Field Note — The Anti-Spoof Check

Every inbound packet's claimed source IP is checked against the sending peer's cryptographic identity before it reaches the TUN device. A peer cannot forge traffic that appears to originate from another member of the mesh.

03

How State Is Held

No global lock. State is split by how often it changes and how sensitive it is to delay, and each piece gets the concurrency primitive that fits.

Fig. 4 — Concurrency Primitives by Component
Three primitives, three access patterns. The routing table is read on every packet and must never block. The roster changes rarely and tolerates a lock. Interface parameters change rarest of all.
ComponentPrimitiveAccess PatternWhy
PeerTableFastDashMap (ahash)Read on every packetForwarding cannot wait on a lock; ahash resists collision attacks on short keys without cryptographic overhead.
NetworkStateArc<RwLock>Many reads, rare writesStatus queries and IPC handlers read constantly; only the reconverge worker writes, after decrypting a new roster.
tun_name, tun_tasksstd::sync::MutexRare, on interface up/downSimple interior mutability is sufficient; contention is not a concern.
tun_tx (channel handle)ArcSwapSwapped on reconfigurationWait-free pointer swap avoids blocking readers during a network transition.
"The routing table is the one place where latency is not negotiable. Everything else can wait a few milliseconds for a lock. A forwarded packet cannot."
04

Recurring Patterns

Three patterns recur across the codebase, each solving a different coordination problem.

Reactor

Low-level multiplexing, NAT traversal, and connection migration are delegated to iroh, built on tokio and quinn. Every peer event is handled by a reactive task scheduler, not polled.

Actor / Message Passing

Background workers never call into each other's state directly. A reconverge worker reports self-removal or promotion to the main IPC loop through mpsc channels, keeping task boundaries clean.

Strategy at the Crate Boundary

The tetron-proto crate defines wire messages (ControlMsg, GroupBlob); the tetron crate supplies OS-specific bindings — TUN setup, routes, privilege gates.

Field Note — No Plugins, By Design

Tetron runs with root privileges to configure network interfaces. It has no plugin system. Packet filtering is left to the host firewall; name resolution, to the host's own configuration; file transfer, to standard tools. Removing an extension surface removes a class of privilege-escalation bugs along with it.

05

Requirements, Traced

Four constraints from the design specification map directly onto specific blocks. Each is satisfied by construction, not by convention.

RequirementDescriptionSatisfied By
SUBNET-001Configurable subnetNetworkState parses and verifies a signed, custom subnet CIDR from the network's GroupBlob.
RENAME-M02Product identityMeshManager binds to its own socket path, service unit, and ALPN prefix (tetron/net/...).
MULTISEG-002Data-plane isolationNetworkHandle gives each joined network its own tasks, TUN device, and peer table — disjoint from every other network.
LIVE-001Invite-only admissionNetworkState validates new members against DHT-published, single-use invite keys before adding them to the roster.
End of Survey

One daemon, many isolated networks, no global lock, no plugin surface. Packets move on channels; state is read far more than it is written; the routing table never waits.
